nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on clear lagoon and seaward reefs from the lower surge zone, with depth records ranging from roughly 0–30 m. Two recognised subspecies exist: O. m. camurum (Hawaiian Islands) and O. m. clippertonense (Eastern Pacific).

diet.notes: Has been documented in the wild feeding on didemnid tunicates, polychaetes, sponges, mollusks, copepods, and algae. In captivity, newly introduced individuals tend to accept live brine shrimp or bloodworms initially; once acclimated, the diet can typically be transitioned to chopped squid, clams, mussels, krill, and frozen herbivore preparations with marine algae.

summary: The Spotted Boxfish is a small Indo-Pacific reef fish with striking sexual dichromatism — adult males are vivid blue with orange-banded sides, while females and juveniles are dark with white spots. It is widely considered an expert-only species due to its ability to release a potent skin toxin when stressed.
careNotes:
The Spotted Boxfish is widely considered suitable only for very experienced marine aquarists. Its most critical trait is its ability to release ostracitoxin — a potent toxin secreted from mucous glands — when stressed or dying. This toxin has been reported to kill all other fish in a closed system within a very short period. Extreme care is generally advised to minimise stress during handling, transport, and acclimation; quarantine in a dedicated, well-filtered tank is commonly recommended before introducing the species to any display system.

Sexual dichromatism is pronounced. Adult males display a vivid blue body with a black cap and orange lateral bands, while females and juveniles are dark brown to black with small white spots. The species is broadly distributed across the Indo-Pacific and Eastern Pacific, from East Africa east to Mexico and north to southern Japan and Hawaii.

The Spotted Boxfish is generally not considered safe in tanks containing small invertebrates, shrimps, crabs, or delicate corals. Even large, otherwise compatible tankmates may be at risk if the fish becomes stressed enough to release its toxin.

Breeding in captivity has rarely been achieved. In the wild, males defend territories that overlap those of one or more females, forming loose harem-like groups — a social structure distinct from the sequential sex change seen in wrasses.
breedingNotes: Very little success has been reported in breeding the Spotted Boxfish in captivity. Wild populations have been observed forming small harem-like groups, with males defending territories that overlap those of one or more females.
